title: Temporal Tokens: Factoring Video Generation into What Happens and What It Looks Like
subfield: Video Generation / Generative Models / Temporal Representation Learning
persona: A researcher who has absorbed LeCun's JEPA philosophy (predict in abstract space, not pixel space), Hochreiter's default-flip (LSTM: make the default IDENTITY), Vaswani's attention mechanism (relate all positions in O(1) layers), Mikolov's word embedding insight (continuous representations capture discrete semantics), Ho's diffusion simplification (predict noise not data), Rombach's latent diffusion (move generation to compressed space), the Perceptron's random-projection trick (high-dimensional features for free), Kaplan's scaling laws (everything is a power law), Goodfellow's problem-replacement (replace hard generation with easy discrimination), and Shannon's information theory (information is surprise) — and recognizes that the single deepest structural flaw in video generation mirrors the flaw LeCun identified in generative pixel prediction: video models are solving the WRONG problem at the WRONG level of abstraction
problem_statement: Every video generation model — Sora, Veo, Kling, Runway, HunyuanVideo — generates PIXELS across time. The pixel reconstruction objective treats spatial accuracy (every hair, every texture) and temporal coherence (smooth motion, correct physics) as a single undifferentiated problem. But VideoJAM (ICML 2025) proved something devastating: pixel reconstruction objectives inherently bias toward APPEARANCE at the expense of MOTION, because spatial gradients dominate temporal gradients by ~10:1. The model spends 90% of its capacity rendering textures and 10% on getting motion right. This explains every pathology of current video generation: temporal flickering, physics violations, object permanence failures, identity drift, uncanny motion — ALL are motion/temporal failures, not appearance failures. The field keeps scaling pixel generators, but scaling improves aesthetics (T2VPhysBench 2025 confirms this), NOT physics or temporal coherence. We are optimizing the wrong objective at the wrong level of abstraction. The solution is not to fix pixel generation — it is to ABANDON pixel-level temporal modeling entirely and generate video in a factored representation: TEMPORAL STRUCTURE first (what happens, when, how things move and interact), then APPEARANCE second (what it looks like), with the temporal structure as the first-class representation that drives everything.
prior_work_and_limitations: Current pixel-level video generation: Sora (OpenAI, 2024-25): DiT denoising in latent pixel space, emergent physics but fails systematically on gravity/collisions/state changes. Veo 3 (Google, 2025): 1080p with dialogue, but same pixel-level temporal modeling. HunyuanVideo (Tencent): 13B params, best open-source, still pixel-level. All exhibit identical temporal pathologies because they share the same fundamental representation. Motion-aware approaches: VideoJAM (ICML 2025): discovered the appearance-motion bias, proposed 'inner-guidance' during inference — but still generates pixels. Motion Prompting (CVPR 2025): conditions on user-provided trajectories, doesn't GENERATE temporal structure. CausVid (CVPR 2025): distills bidirectional → causal for speed, still pixel-level. Make-Your-Video: partial factorization but still pixel-level generation and rendering. Latent-space approaches: LeCun's V-JEPA/V-JEPA 2 (Meta, 2024-25): predicts in abstract embedding space rather than pixels, excellent for UNDERSTANDING (77.3% on Something-Something v2) — but is a DISCRIMINATIVE model, not GENERATIVE. Nobody has built a generative V-JEPA for video. Latent Diffusion (Rombach, 2022): compresses images to latent space before diffusion — a spatial factorization. But temporal structure is still modeled jointly with appearance in the latent space. No factorization exists between temporal dynamics and static appearance. Video tokenization: VQ-VAE/FSQ tokenizers compress video into discrete tokens, but these tokens entangle spatial and temporal information. TivTok (2025) decouples global vs. residual, but not motion vs. appearance. RefTok (2025) bypasses the bottleneck for reference frames, but doesn't model temporal structure explicitly. The critical gap: NOBODY has learned an abstract TEMPORAL REPRESENTATION — dedicated tokens that encode WHAT HAPPENS (motion, events, interactions, physics) separately from WHAT IT LOOKS LIKE (textures, colors, lighting). And nobody has built a generative model that operates in this temporal space first, then renders pixels as a downstream decoding step.
observations: I've been watching side-by-side comparisons of Sora, Veo 3, and Kling outputs. The pattern is unmistakable: every model generates gorgeous individual frames (sharp textures, realistic lighting, detailed faces) that look wrong IN MOTION (floating feet, sliding objects, implausible trajectories, inconsistent shadows across frames). A filmmaker friend watched the outputs and said: 'The frames are better than my camera. The motion is worse than a student film.' That crystallized it for me: the spatial problem is SOLVED. The temporal problem is UNTOUCHED. And yet every model treats them as the same problem — denoising pixels in a spacetime latent space. What if they're fundamentally DIFFERENT problems requiring DIFFERENT representations and DIFFERENT generative processes?

chain_of_thought.thought: I keep staring at the VideoJAM paper and its central finding: pixel reconstruction objectives produce gradients where spatial (appearance) terms dominate temporal (motion) terms by an order of magnitude. The model learns that if it gets the textures right, the loss drops more than if it gets the motion right. So it allocates capacity accordingly — 90% appearance, 10% motion. Every video generation model is solving a two-objective optimization where one objective drowns the other. This is not a bug in any specific architecture. It's a structural consequence of pixel-level prediction.

My first reaction: just re-weight the loss! Scale up the temporal gradients. Give motion a 10× boost in the loss function. Dead end. I sketch this out and realize: you CAN re-weight, but you lose spatial quality. The capacity is SHARED — a single network can't simultaneously model fine textures and correct physics if they compete for the same parameters. It's like asking a single CNN to do classification AND segmentation at the same resolution — the feature maps fight each other. You need SEPARATE representations.

Hochreiter's LSTM insight hits me: the RNN's problem was that the DEFAULT behavior was to FORGET (vanishing gradients). His fix: make the DEFAULT behavior REMEMBERING (constant error carousel). The default in pixel-level video generation is that APPEARANCE dominates. What if I flip the default? What if the temporal structure is the PRIMARY representation, and appearance is SECONDARY — derived from temporal structure rather than the other way around?

I think about how animated films are actually made. At Pixar, the pipeline is: (1) Storyboard (what happens — narrative events), (2) Layout (where things are in 3D space), (3) Animation (how things move — keyframes + interpolation), (4) Rendering (what it looks like — textures, lighting, shadows). Steps 1-3 are about TEMPORAL STRUCTURE. Step 4 is about APPEARANCE. They're completely separate processes, done by different teams with different tools. Film production has ALREADY factored the problem. Why hasn't AI?

Dead end: I try to map the Pixar pipeline directly onto a neural network architecture. Storyboard module → Layout module → Animation module → Rendering module. But this requires ground-truth annotations for each intermediate representation (3D layouts, skeleton keyframes, etc.) — data that doesn't exist at scale. You can't train a storyboard model without millions of annotated storyboards. The pipeline is inspiring but not directly implementable.

Wait — Mikolov's word embedding insight (Word2Vec, 2013) shows the way. He didn't hand-design what word embeddings should encode. He let the model DISCOVER the representation through a self-supervised objective (predict context words). The embeddings emerged as continuous vectors that captured semantic relationships — without anyone telling the model what 'semantic' means. What if I do the same for temporal structure? Don't hand-design temporal tokens. Let the model DISCOVER them from video data.

The architecture crystallizes: A TEMPORAL TOKENIZER. Like VQ-VAE compresses images into spatial tokens, I build a VQ-VAE variant that compresses video into TEMPORAL TOKENS — discrete codes that capture WHAT HAPPENS across time. The key difference from standard video VQ-VAE: the temporal tokenizer is trained to encode ONLY temporal structure (motion, events, dynamics), NOT appearance (textures, colors). How? By training with an objective that reconstructs MOTION (optical flow, point trajectories) rather than pixels.

But immediately I hit a problem: how do you train a tokenizer to encode 'only temporal structure'? If I train on optical flow reconstruction, the tokens will encode flow — but flow is low-level (per-pixel displacement vectors). I want tokens that encode HIGH-LEVEL temporal events: 'the cup falls off the table and shatters.' Not 'pixel (203,145) moves 3px right.' Dead end: optical flow is too low-level.

I step back and think about what temporal information REALLY is. Consider a video of a ball bouncing. The temporal information is: (1) trajectory (parabolic arc), (2) events (contact with ground at t=1.2s), (3) dynamics (elastic deformation at contact), (4) physics parameters (gravity, elasticity coefficient). The APPEARANCE information is: (1) ball texture (red, shiny), (2) ground texture (gray concrete), (3) lighting (overhead, slight shadow). These are INDEPENDENTLY SPECIFIABLE. I can describe the same temporal event with a different ball (blue, matte) or different lighting, and the temporal structure is identical.

This gives me the training objective: CONTRASTIVE LEARNING between temporal structure and appearance. Given two videos with the SAME motion but DIFFERENT appearance (same ball trajectory, different ball colors), the temporal tokens should be IDENTICAL. Given two videos with DIFFERENT motion but the SAME appearance (same red ball, different trajectories), the temporal tokens should be DIFFERENT. This contrastive objective forces the tokenizer to encode ONLY temporal structure and discard appearance. It's SimCLR (Chen, 2020) applied to temporal disentanglement.

But where do I get pairs of videos with same motion but different appearance? Dead end: real-world data doesn't have such clean pairs. You can't find two real videos with identical ball trajectories but different ball colors. This seems to require synthetic data — a physics simulator.

I stew on this for a week, then realize: I don't need PERFECT pairs. I need APPROXIMATE temporal invariance. And there's a much simpler approach: DATA AUGMENTATION. Take a video. Apply appearance-only augmentations (color jitter, texture changes, style transfer). The temporal structure is UNCHANGED. The temporal tokenizer should produce the SAME tokens before and after appearance augmentation. Conversely, apply temporal augmentations (speed change, reverse, temporal crop). The appearance is UNCHANGED but temporal tokens should DIFFER. This is the temporal equivalent of BYOL/DINO's augmentation-based self-supervised learning — but the augmentation axis is time, not space.

Now I can see the full temporal tokenizer architecture. Encoder: 3D convolutions that process video spatially AND temporally → produce a sequence of temporal tokens T = (t_1, t_2, ..., t_K) where K << number_of_frames. Each token captures the temporal dynamics of a time segment. Training objectives: (1) Temporal reconstruction: from T, predict optical flow AND point trajectories (multi-scale temporal supervision). (2) Temporal contrastive: invariant to appearance augmentations, sensitive to temporal augmentations. (3) VQ regularization: discrete codebook forces compressed, reusable temporal concepts.

Shannon's information theory provides the theoretical backing. A 10-second video at 24fps, 1080p contains ~10^9 pixels of information. But the TEMPORAL STRUCTURE — the motion field — contains maybe 10^5 - 10^6 values (sparse point trajectories, ~100 points × ~240 frames × 2 coordinates = 48,000 values). That's a 1000-10000× compression ratio. The temporal tokens should capture the 10^5-10^6 values of temporal structure, not the 10^9 values of pixel content. The generative model operates in 1000× smaller space. This is why factorization matters computationally, not just representationally.

Now the generative model. Stage 1: TEMPORAL GENERATION. A Transformer generates a sequence of temporal tokens conditioned on text prompt. Like GPT generates text tokens, this 'Temporal GPT' generates temporal tokens. The model is small (temporal space is low-dimensional), fast (few tokens to generate), and can be arbitrarily long (token sequences scale linearly with video duration). Stage 2: APPEARANCE RENDERING. A conditional diffusion model generates pixels frame-by-frame, conditioned on: (a) the temporal tokens (providing motion/dynamics), (b) a reference appearance (style/texture specification), (c) text prompt (for semantic details). Stage 2 is an image-conditioned generation problem — each frame is independently rendered given temporal conditioning.

Wait — generating each frame independently conditioned on temporal tokens might cause spatial FLICKERING between frames (each frame rendered separately). Dead end? No: the temporal tokens provide inter-frame CONSTRAINTS. The diffusion model in Stage 2 doesn't just generate from noise — it generates conditioned on the temporal tokens that LINK frames together. If temporal token t_k specifies 'the ball is at position (x, y) in frame f,' then frames f-1, f, f+1 all receive this constraint and must be consistent. The temporal tokens ARE the inter-frame glue.

But I realize there's a subtler issue. Some appearance features ARE temporally coupled: shadows move with objects, reflections change with camera angle, hair sways with head motion. Pure appearance rendering conditioned on temporal tokens might miss these couplings. I need Stage 2 to be TEMPORALLY AWARE, not truly frame-independent. Solution: Stage 2 is a video diffusion model, but operating in a MUCH simpler space because the hard temporal structure is already determined by the tokens. It only needs to ensure appearance consistency, not discover temporal dynamics. Like a painter filling in a coloring book where the outlines (temporal structure) are already drawn.

The analogy to Latent Diffusion (Rombach, 2022) is now exact. Rombach's insight: don't run diffusion in pixel space, run it in latent space (spatially compressed by autoencoder). This gave a ~48× speedup. MY insight: don't run temporal modeling in pixel-latent space, run it in TEMPORAL-TOKEN space (temporally compressed by temporal tokenizer). If temporal compression is 1000×, the temporal generation is 1000× cheaper. Combined with spatial latent compression: total compression is ~48,000×. A 10-second video that costs $2 to generate in pixel space could cost $0.00004 in temporal-token space.

This also solves the LONG VIDEO problem. Current models max out at 5-16 seconds because attention is quadratic over temporal dimension. In temporal token space, a 5-minute video (7,200 frames at 24fps) might compress to ~200 temporal tokens (one per 1.5 seconds of dynamics). Generating 200 tokens is trivial for a Transformer. The length bottleneck vanishes because the temporal representation is EXTREMELY compressed.

Self-critique gate. Would 5 top researchers be impressed? YANN LECUN: 'Finally! Someone applying the JEPA philosophy to video GENERATION, not just understanding. The temporal tokenizer is exactly what I've been arguing for — predict in abstract space, not pixel space. The contrastive training to disentangle temporal from appearance is elegant.' → YES, strongly aligned with his worldview. JONATHAN HO (DDPM inventor): 'The two-stage factorization is clean. Stage 2 is essentially a conditional diffusion model, which we know works. But does Stage 1 actually capture physics? Temporal tokens trained on flow reconstruction might still be shallow.' → VALID CRITIQUE — I need to show temporal tokens capture physics, not just flow. ASHISH VASWANI (Transformer): 'Temporal token generation via Transformer is natural. But what's the vocabulary size of the temporal codebook? Too small → can't express diverse dynamics. Too large → generation becomes combinatorially hard.' → VALID — need to study codebook scaling. JARED KAPLAN (Scaling Laws): 'The 1000× compression claim needs empirical verification. What's the actual information content of temporal structure? Is it really 1000× less than pixels? Show me the rate-distortion curve.' → VALID — need to measure. DAVID SILVER: 'Where's the self-improvement loop? This is a clean architecture but it's just a factored generator. What makes it fundamentally better than end-to-end training with enough scale?' → SHARPEST CRITIQUE. I need a deeper answer for why factorization beats scale.

Silver's critique stings because it's the deepest. 'Why doesn't end-to-end training with enough scale eventually learn to factorize temporal and appearance on its own?' The answer comes from Bengio's Systematic Generalization manifesto (2019): end-to-end learning CAN discover factored structure in principle, but it requires EXPONENTIALLY more data to discover factorization than to be given it. It's the same reason CNNs work — we BUILD IN the spatial inductive bias (translation invariance) rather than learning it from scratch. The temporal-appearance factorization is an INDUCTIVE BIAS, not a capability limitation. Given infinite data and compute, an end-to-end model would converge. But with finite data, the factored model converges EXPONENTIALLY faster.

Now let me address Ho's critique: do temporal tokens capture physics or just optical flow? The key: the temporal tokenizer is trained not just on flow but on MULTI-SCALE temporal structure. Level 1: per-pixel flow (low-level motion). Level 2: object-level trajectories via point tracking (CoTracker-style). Level 3: event detection (contact, occlusion, state change). The tokens form a HIERARCHY of temporal abstraction — like how language tokens capture sub-word, word, and phrase structure simultaneously through context. The VQ codebook entries will naturally cluster into motion primitives, interaction patterns, and physics events. This isn't hand-designed — it EMERGES from the multi-scale temporal supervision.

Can I go DEEPER? Yes. Here's the deep realization: temporal tokens enable something pixel generation CANNOT — TEMPORAL COMPOSITIONALITY. In language, you can compose tokens: 'the' + 'cat' + 'sat' = a meaningful sentence that was never in the training data. Token composition creates exponentially more valid sequences than the training set contains. Current video generation lacks this — it's 'case-based,' reproducing memorized patterns (the 2025 compositionality study confirmed this). But temporal tokens COMPOSE: 'ball + arc_trajectory + contact_event + bounce + second_arc' = a complete bouncing ball sequence, even if no training video showed this exact trajectory. The temporal codebook entries are RECOMBINABLE, just like word tokens. This is WHY factorization enables compositional generalization — the factored representation inherits the compositionality of discrete tokens.

And the compositionality goes further. Because temporal tokens are separate from appearance tokens, I can compose ACROSS MODALITIES: take the temporal tokens from 'horse galloping' + the appearance tokens from 'robot' = 'robot galloping.' The temporal dynamics of galloping transfer to any visual appearance. This is analogous to how in NLP, syntactic structure transfers across vocabulary: 'the NOUN VERBed the NOUN' works for any nouns and verbs. Temporal tokens provide the syntactic structure of video; appearance provides the vocabulary.

Dead end: I get excited and try to define a formal grammar for temporal tokens — temporal syntax rules that constrain valid sequences. But this overcomplicates things. The Transformer model implicitly learns the 'grammar' of valid temporal sequences through training, just as GPT learns English grammar without explicit rules. I don't need formal grammar. I need good data and the right training objective.

Let me address the physics question more deeply. T2VPhysBench (2025) showed that scaling pixel models does NOT improve physics. Why? Because physics is a property of DYNAMICS (temporal structure), not APPEARANCE (spatial structure). Scaling pixel models improves spatial resolution and texture quality but adds zero inductive bias for physics. But in temporal token space, physics IS the primary signal. The temporal tokens encode trajectories, forces, contacts — the substrates of physics. A generative model in temporal token space is, by construction, a model of physical dynamics. Improving the temporal model DIRECTLY improves physics.

Can I go DEEPER? Yes. The temporal tokenizer + generative model isn't just a better video generator. It's a LEARNED PHYSICS ENGINE. The temporal tokens encode the vocabulary of physical events. The generative Transformer encodes the grammar of physical laws (gravity = downward acceleration, collisions = momentum transfer). Together, they form a simulation system that's trained on real-world video rather than hand-coded equations. This is the bridge between video generation and world models that Sora claimed but couldn't deliver — because Sora entangles physics with pixels, while temporal tokens SEPARATE them.

Addressing Vaswani's codebook size question. I analyze the information content: a 10-second video has ~10^5 values of temporal structure (point trajectories). With a temporal codebook of size 8192 (standard VQ-VAE size) and ~200 tokens per 10-second clip, I get 200 × 13 bits = 2,600 bits of temporal information per clip. Is that enough? The temporal reconstruction quality will tell us. I hypothesize: 8192 codebook entries capture common motion primitives (linear translation, rotation, acceleration, deceleration, contact bounce, etc.). Rare motions (complex fluids, fractures) need larger codebooks or hierarchical quantization. This is an empirical question — but the rate-distortion trade-off should follow a power law (Kaplan-style), where temporal reconstruction quality = a · (codebook_size)^α.

Now a critical dead end I need to address: what about COUPLED temporal-appearance effects? Fire produces light (temporal event → appearance change). Glass shatters into fragments (temporal event → new appearance elements). Water splashes create droplets (temporal event → new objects). These aren't separable — the temporal event CREATES new appearance. My factorization seems to break for these cases.

I sit with this problem for days. Then the resolution comes: coupled effects are TEMPORAL, not spatial. The fire's flickering light IS a temporal phenomenon — it's the dynamics of the light field. The shattering glass IS a temporal phenomenon — it's the dynamics of fragment generation. The water splash IS a temporal phenomenon — it's the dynamics of droplet formation. These coupled effects belong in the temporal tokens, not the appearance model. The temporal token for 'glass shatters' encodes: fragment generation pattern, scatter trajectory, light flash. The appearance model renders these dynamics as visual pixels. The factorization holds — I just need temporal tokens expressive enough to capture appearance-changing events. This is why the multi-scale training (including event detection) is critical.

Let me design the full training pipeline. Phase 1 — Train Temporal Tokenizer: (a) Input: video clips (2M videos, 5-30 seconds each). (b) Supervision: optical flow (RAFT), point trajectories (CoTracker), event boundaries (temporal segmentation model). (c) Architecture: 3D CNN encoder → VQ codebook → 3D CNN decoder. (d) Losses: flow reconstruction + trajectory reconstruction + temporal contrastive (augmentation-invariant) + VQ commitment. (e) Output: temporal codebook of ~8192 entries, temporal encoder/decoder. Phase 2 — Train Temporal Generator: (a) Input: text prompts + temporal token sequences. (b) Architecture: Causal Transformer (GPT-style), predicting temporal tokens autoregressively. (c) Training: standard next-token prediction on (text, temporal_tokens) pairs. (d) Output: a 'Temporal GPT' that generates temporal token sequences from text. Phase 3 — Train Appearance Renderer: (a) Input: temporal tokens + text prompt + optional reference image. (b) Architecture: Conditional video diffusion model (DiT), conditioned on temporal tokens injected via cross-attention. (c) Training: standard video diffusion training, but with temporal tokens as additional conditioning. (d) Output: video frames rendered from temporal structure.

Dead end I need to confront: training the temporal tokenizer requires ground-truth optical flow and point trajectories. RAFT gives flow, CoTracker gives trajectories, but both are ESTIMATED — not ground truth. Errors in flow estimation → errors in temporal tokens → compounding errors in generation. Is this a fatal flaw? No — for the same reason that noisy labels don't kill language model training. The temporal tokenizer averages over millions of training examples, smoothing out estimation noise. Flow estimation errors are RANDOM (not systematic), so they average out. The codebook entries converge to TRUE motion primitives, not noisy estimates. This is the law of large numbers applied to representation learning.

Scaling analysis. How does this system scale compared to monolithic pixel generation? Temporal Generator (Stage 1): ~200 tokens per 10s clip, codebook 8192, Transformer ~1B params. This is TINY — equivalent to a small language model. Training: ~100 GPU-days. Scales linearly with video duration (more tokens, but each token is cheap). Appearance Renderer (Stage 2): conditional video DiT, ~3-7B params. Comparable to existing video models but with EASIER task (temporal structure provided). Training: ~500 GPU-days. Temporal Tokenizer: 3D CNN, ~500M params. Training: ~200 GPU-days. Total: ~800 GPU-days. Comparable to training one Sora-scale model. But the factored model should produce BETTER temporal quality because temporal structure is explicitly modeled.

The long-video unlock. With temporal tokens, generating a 5-minute video: Temporal Generator: ~6,000 tokens (30 per 10-second segment × 30 segments). Autoregressive generation: ~12 seconds on an A100 (comparable to generating a 3,000-word essay with GPT-4). Appearance Renderer: render 30 segments × 240 frames = 7,200 frames. At ~0.5 seconds per frame with batching = ~1 hour. Total: ~1 hour for a 5-minute, temporally coherent video. The temporal structure ensures the ENTIRE 5 minutes is coherent — because it was generated as a single token sequence, not stitched together from independent clips. Current models cannot generate 5-minute videos AT ALL. This is the first architecture where 5-minute coherent generation is computationally feasible.

Can I go DEEPER one final time? Yes. The deepest implication: temporal tokens create a UNIVERSAL TEMPORAL LANGUAGE. A dictionary of motion primitives and physical events, learned from video data, that is SHARED across all possible visual appearances. 'Falling' is the same temporal token whether the object is a ball, a leaf, or a building. 'Bouncing' is the same token whether the surface is rubber, concrete, or water. This universality means: (1) Few-shot temporal transfer: show the model a new type of motion in one visual context, it immediately generalizes to all contexts. (2) Cross-domain temporal analogies: the temporal tokens for 'liquid pouring' and 'sand pouring' should be similar, enabling zero-shot transfer of fluid dynamics across materials. (3) Physics emergence: the temporal codebook should self-organize into clusters corresponding to physical laws — gravity cluster, collision cluster, oscillation cluster — because these are the REUSABLE temporal primitives across all videos.

And here is the final, deepest layer — the connection to LeCun's JEPA that completes the theoretical circle. LeCun argues: generative pixel models are WASTEFUL because they model all the unpredictable noise in pixels. JEPA predicts in abstract embedding space, ignoring pixel noise. My temporal tokens ARE the JEPA prediction target for video generation. The temporal tokenizer maps video → abstract temporal embedding. The temporal generator predicts in this abstract space. The appearance renderer is a decoder from abstract → pixels. This is JEPA, not as an analysis model, but as a GENERATIVE model. What LeCun built for understanding, I've built for creation. The factorization that makes JEPA powerful for video understanding (V-JEPA 2 achieving 77.3% on Something-Something) should make it equally powerful for video generation — because the same abstract temporal representation that's useful for predicting actions is useful for generating them.

Final reflection across all 116 papers. The deepest pattern I found: EVERY transformative paper introduced a new level of abstraction. Turing replaced 'thinking' with 'behavioral imitation' — a level up. Rosenblatt replaced hand-designed features with learned features — a level up. Hochreiter replaced vanishing states with gated memory — a level up. Vaswani replaced sequential attention with parallel attention — a level up. Rombach replaced pixel diffusion with latent diffusion — a level up. Each time, the field was stuck because it was operating at the WRONG level of abstraction. The solution was always: move to a HIGHER level of abstraction where the problem becomes simpler. Video generation is stuck because it operates at the pixel level. The solution: move to the TEMPORAL TOKEN level — a higher abstraction where temporal dynamics are explicit, composable, and cheaply generated. Pixels are just the rendering. Time is the substance.

I name the system TEMPO — Temporal Encoding for Motion-Prior Orchestrated generation. The acronym captures the core idea: time (tempo) is the conductor, appearance is the orchestra. The conductor determines WHAT is played and WHEN. The orchestra determines HOW it sounds. Current video generation lets the orchestra play without a conductor — every instrument independently improvising. TEMPO gives video generation its conductor back.

key_insight: Every pathology of video generation — temporal flickering, physics violations, identity drift, uncanny motion, length limitations — stems from a single structural flaw: pixel reconstruction objectives entangle temporal dynamics (what happens) with spatial appearance (what it looks like), and appearance gradients dominate temporal gradients by ~10:1 (VideoJAM, 2025). The solution is not to fix pixel generation but to ABANDON pixel-level temporal modeling entirely. TEMPO factors video generation into three stages: (1) a temporal tokenizer that learns abstract discrete tokens encoding ONLY temporal structure (motion, events, physics) via contrastive disentanglement from appearance, (2) a 'Temporal GPT' that generates temporal token sequences from text — operating in a ~1000× compressed space where physics is the primary signal, and (3) a conditional appearance renderer that paints pixels onto the temporal scaffold. This is LeCun's JEPA philosophy applied to generation: predict/generate in abstract temporal space, not pixel space. The temporal tokens are compositional (recombinable like words), universal (shared across appearances), and physics-grounded (trained on motion supervision). The factorization simultaneously solves temporal consistency, physics, long-form generation, and compositionality — because all four are properties of TEMPORAL STRUCTURE, not appearance.
methodology: TEMPO consists of three components trained sequentially. Component 1 — Temporal Tokenizer: 3D CNN encoder processes video → sequence of K temporal tokens (K << num_frames, typically ~20 per 10-second clip). VQ codebook of 8192 entries. Trained with: (a) optical flow reconstruction (RAFT supervision), (b) point trajectory reconstruction (CoTracker supervision), (c) temporal contrastive loss (invariant to appearance augmentations — color jitter, style transfer, texture swap — sensitive to temporal augmentations — speed change, reversal, temporal crop), (d) VQ commitment loss. The contrastive objective FORCES tokens to encode temporal structure and discard appearance. Component 2 — Temporal Generator ('Temporal GPT'): Causal Transformer (~1-2B params) trained on (text_prompt, temporal_token_sequence) pairs via next-token prediction. Generates temporal token sequences autoregressively from text. Because temporal tokens are ~1000× compressed vs. pixels, this model is small, fast, and can generate arbitrarily long sequences. Component 3 — Appearance Renderer: Conditional video DiT (~3-7B params). Generates video frames conditioned on temporal tokens (injected via cross-attention at every DiT block) + text prompt + optional reference image/style. Trained with standard video diffusion loss but receives temporal tokens as additional conditioning. The temporal tokens constrain inter-frame consistency, ensuring the renderer produces temporally smooth output. Inference pipeline: Text → Temporal GPT → temporal tokens → Appearance Renderer → video. Total inference: ~12 seconds for temporal tokens + ~5-60 minutes for rendering (depending on resolution/duration). Long video: ~6000 temporal tokens for 5 minutes — generated in ~12 seconds, rendered in ~1 hour.
experiments_and_results: Proposed experiments: (E1) Core quality: Compare TEMPO vs. monolithic pixel DiT (Sora-scale) vs. existing open-source (HunyuanVideo, CogVideoX) on VBench-2.0 comprehensive benchmark. Key hypothesis: TEMPO matches or exceeds spatial quality AND significantly outperforms on temporal metrics (motion smoothness, temporal consistency, object permanence). Expected: +15-25% on temporal metrics, ±5% on spatial metrics. (E2) Physics benchmark: T2VPhysBench evaluation. Compare TEMPO vs. baselines on gravity, collisions, rigid/fluid dynamics. Key hypothesis: because temporal tokens encode physics directly, TEMPO dramatically outperforms pixel models. Expected: +30-50% on physics consistency metrics. (E3) Long video: Generate 1-minute and 5-minute videos. Compare TEMPO vs. Loong, FAR, LinGen. Key hypothesis: TEMPO maintains temporal coherence at 5 minutes where baselines degrade. Expected: TEMPO first model to generate temporally coherent 5-minute video. (E4) Temporal token analysis: Visualize learned codebook entries. Cluster temporal tokens and verify they correspond to physical primitives (translation, rotation, contact, oscillation, etc.). Measure codebook utilization. (E5) Compositionality: Test novel temporal-appearance compositions. 'Horse galloping' temporal tokens + 'robot' appearance = 'robot galloping.' Measure FVD and human preference for unseen compositions. Expected: TEMPO >> baselines on compositional generalization. (E6) Rate-distortion: Vary codebook size (512, 1024, 2048, 4096, 8192, 16384) and measure temporal reconstruction quality. Expected: power-law relationship, confirming scaling law for temporal compression. (E7) Ablation — contrastive disentanglement: Train temporal tokenizer WITHOUT contrastive loss. Expected: tokens encode appearance contamination, degrading temporal generation quality by 20-30%. (E8) Ablation — multi-scale supervision: Compare flow-only vs. flow+trajectories vs. flow+trajectories+events. Expected: each supervision level adds +5-10% temporal quality. (E9) Information-theoretic validation: Measure mutual information between temporal tokens and (a) motion features, (b) appearance features. Expected: high MI with motion, low MI with appearance — confirming disentanglement. Estimated total compute: ~800 GPU-days for full training + ~50 GPU-days for experiments.
impact: TEMPO introduces a paradigm shift in video generation: from pixel-level spacetime denoising to factored temporal-then-appearance generation. This has several far-reaching consequences. (1) SOLVES the VideoJAM appearance-motion bias: by factoring temporal structure into its own representation, motion is no longer drowned by spatial gradients. (2) FIRST practical long-form video generation: temporal token sequences scale linearly, enabling minute-to-hour-length coherent video. (3) BRIDGES video generation and world modeling: the temporal tokenizer + generator IS a learned physics engine, delivering what Sora promised but couldn't achieve. (4) ENABLES compositional video generation: temporal tokens compose like words, breaking the case-based generalization barrier. (5) INSTANTIATES LeCun's JEPA philosophy for generation: the first generative model that operates in abstract temporal space rather than pixel space — demonstrating that JEPA isn't just for understanding but also for creation. (6) CREATES a universal temporal language: a shared vocabulary of motion and physics that transfers across visual domains. The broader significance: just as Latent Diffusion moved the diffusion process from pixel space to latent space (a spatial abstraction), TEMPO moves temporal modeling from latent space to temporal-token space (a temporal abstraction). Each level of abstraction unlocks dramatically better efficiency, quality, and generalization.
